items.correct: Delocalised electrons are free to move through the lattice of positive ions, carrying charge.
items.misconception: Metal atoms themselves move to carry the current.
items.random_wrong: Metals contain naturally moving protons that act as charge carriers.
items.why: Students picture conduction as 'something moving through the wire' and default to whole atoms, since they don't fully grasp delocalisation.

items.q: Where is almost all the mass of an atom located?
items.correct: In the nucleus, made up of protons and neutrons.
items.misconception: Electrons contribute significantly to the mass of an atom.
items.random_wrong: Mass is spread evenly throughout the entire atom including the electron shells.
items.why: Students see electrons listed alongside protons and neutrons and assume they contribute comparable mass — in reality an electron has ~1/1836 the mass of a proton.

items.q: What is different between two isotopes of the same element?
items.correct: They have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
items.misconception: Isotopes have different numbers of electrons, which is why their mass differs.
items.random_wrong: Isotopes are atoms of different elements with the same mass.
items.why: Students conflate isotopes with ions because both alter a property of the atom — but ions change electrons (charge), isotopes change neutrons (mass).

items.q: Why are alloys usually harder than the pure metals they're made from?
items.correct: Different-sized atoms disrupt the layers, so they can't slide easily
items.misconception: The extra atoms add covalent bonds
items.why: Correct answer: Different-sized atoms disrupt the layers, so they can't slide easily. “The extra atoms add covalent bonds” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Why do nanoparticles make such good catalysts?
items.correct: They have a huge surface area to volume ratio
items.misconception: They have a very small surface area
items.why: The answer examiners want is: They have a huge surface area to volume ratio. “They have a very small surface area” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A cubic nanoparticle has sides of 5 nm. What is its surface area in nm²?
items.why: Exact answer: 150. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
